lagos-lagos.kidnappingNote: Lagos sees occasional kidnap-for-ransom and abduction cases, mostly opportunistic rather than the organized banditry seen in North West Nigeria.
lagos-lagos.terrorismNote: No significant insurgency activity, but isolated security alerts occur in a city this size.
lagos-lagos.policeConductNote: Lagos was the site of the most severe single incident of the 2020 #EndSARS crackdown — the Lekki toll gate shooting, where Amnesty International says security forces killed at least 12 peaceful protesters.

abuja-fct.kidnappingNote: Abuja itself is comparatively well-policed, but its highway approaches (notably the Abuja-Kaduna road) are among the most dangerous kidnapping corridors in Nigeria.
abuja-fct.terrorismNote: The FCT has tightened security since past attacks; isolated alerts still occur near its borders with Niger and Kaduna States.
abuja-fct.policeConductNote: Abuja saw #EndSARS protests in 2020 with reports of excessive force, including tear gas and live rounds used against demonstrators.

port-harcourt-rivers.kidnappingNote: Rivers State has a long-running history of kidnap-for-ransom tied to the oil and gas sector, alongside cult-related violence.
port-harcourt-rivers.policeConductNote: Port Harcourt saw #EndSARS protests and crackdowns in 2020 alongside other major cities.

kano-kano.kidnappingNote: Kano State, part of the North West zone, records armed robbery and occasional kidnapping; the city itself has seen reduced extremist activity in recent years compared to the early 2010s.

kaduna-kaduna.kidnappingNote: Kaduna State recorded 629 kidnap victims between July 2024 and June 2025 — the second-highest of any Nigerian state — driven by banditry and intercommunal violence, especially along the Abuja-Kaduna and Birnin Gwari roads.
kaduna-kaduna.terrorismNote: Parts of southern Kaduna State have seen banditry sometimes described as terrorism-adjacent violence.

jos-plateau.terrorismNote: Plateau State has a history of intercommunal and religious clashes, though Jos itself has been calmer in recent years.

owerri-imo.kidnappingNote: Imo State has seen periodic kidnapping and unrest tied to separatist agitation in the South East.

awka-anambra.kidnappingNote: Anambra State has experienced kidnapping and unrest tied to separatist agitation and cult violence in the wider South East.

onitsha-anambra.kidnappingNote: Onitsha has a reputation for armed robbery, kidnapping, and cult-related violence linked to its dense commercial activity.

aba-abia.kidnappingNote: Aba has documented issues with robbery, cultism, and periodic unrest as a dense commercial city.

warri-delta.kidnappingNote: Warri has a documented history of armed robbery, kidnapping, cult activity, and ethnic/community clashes tied to oil-sector resource tension in the Niger Delta.

bauchi-bauchi.terrorismNote: Bauchi State borders the North East insurgency zone and has experienced Boko Haram-linked attacks historically, though less severely than Borno or Yobe.

maiduguri-borno.kidnappingNote: Abductions by Boko Haram/ISWAP have been a recurring feature of the insurgency in and around Maiduguri.
maiduguri-borno.terrorismNote: Maiduguri is widely cited as Nigeria's most dangerous city due to the prolonged Boko Haram/ISWAP insurgency, which has caused over 38,000 deaths in Borno State alone since it began. ISWAP escalated attacks again in 2025, including drone strikes on military positions.

damaturu-yobe.terrorismNote: Yobe State has suffered repeated Boko Haram bombings and attacks; a September 2024 ISWAP attack killed at least 170 villagers in Mafa ward, Yobe.

yola-adamawa.terrorismNote: Adamawa State has experienced Boko Haram attacks on villages, markets, and churches, though Yola itself has been comparatively more stable than Maiduguri or Damaturu.

gombe-gombe.terrorismNote: Gombe State neighbors the North East insurgency zone but has seen less direct attack activity than Borno or Yobe in recent years.

sokoto-sokoto.kidnappingNote: Sokoto State recorded 358 kidnap victims between July 2024 and June 2025, one of the higher totals in the North West banditry belt.

katsina-katsina.kidnappingNote: Katsina State recorded 566 kidnap victims between July 2024 and June 2025, among the highest totals nationally, driven by banditry in rural areas.

gusau-zamfara.kidnappingNote: Zamfara State recorded 1,203 kidnap victims between July 2024 and June 2025 — the highest of any Nigerian state — due to entrenched banditry.
gusau-zamfara.terrorismNote: Banditry in Zamfara is sometimes described as terrorism-adjacent given its scale and organization.

minna-niger.kidnappingNote: Niger State forms part of the Kaduna-Niger-Zamfara forest belt that bandit groups use as a base, contributing to elevated kidnapping risk along its highways.

birnin-kebbi-kebbi.kidnappingNote: Kebbi is part of the North West zone, which accounts for over 60% of Nigeria's reported kidnapping cases, though Kebbi's own totals are lower than Zamfara, Kaduna, or Katsina.

zaria-kaduna.kidnappingNote: Zaria shares Kaduna State's elevated kidnapping risk (629 state-wide victims between July 2024 and June 2025).

makurdi-benue.kidnappingNote: Benue State has experienced repeated farmer-herder clashes alongside general kidnapping risk.

dutse-jigawa.kidnappingNote: Jigawa is part of the North West zone, which accounts for over 60% of Nigeria's reported kidnapping cases nationally.

jalingo-taraba.terrorismNote: Southern Taraba has occasionally seen spillover violence from neighboring conflict zones.
